JavaScript Core Concepts (1): How JavaScript Actually Runs
This is the first post in the series, and we're going to look at how JavaScript actually runs.

There's one important thing to get straight right at the start: JavaScript can't be run by your computer directly. It has to go through a middleman — an interpreter — that translates the instructions into something the computer can understand.
This line has to be translated by the interpreter first before the computer can run it.
So what's an interpreted language? JavaScript is one. An interpreted language is, well, a language that gets compiled on the fly (yeah, kind of a circular definition, I know).
The other kind is a compiled language. The big thing about compiled languages is that
while you're writing the code, it's already being compiled ahead of time
. So when there's a problem, you can catch the bug early. That's the upside of a compiled language, and generally the performance is better too.

So what does "compiled ahead of time" actually mean, and why does it give better performance? The reason is that when you write the code, it's already turned into machine code (the instructions the computer understands), so it can be run directly. The big difference with an interpreted language is that it has to read the code one line at a time and convert it into machine code as it goes. So by comparison, compiled languages perform better.
The downside of compiled languages, though, is that you have less flexibility while developing, and usually the dev speed and the time spent debugging are longer than with an interpreted language.

So let's circle back to interpreted languages. Like I mentioned, the most obvious difference between an interpreted language and a compiled one is that an interpreted language has to read the code line by line and convert it to machine code through the interpreter before it can run. Because of that, error messages in an interpreted language usually show up right in your development environment — for JavaScript, for example, they show up straight in the browser's console.

The upside of an interpreted language is that development is faster and more flexible. In JavaScript, for instance, you don't have to define types up front — the interpreter figures out the type dynamically.
How the JavaScript Interpreter Translates Your Code
How the JavaScript interpreter translates your code

Like I said earlier, an interpreted language reads the code one line at a time and converts it into machine code through the interpreter. JavaScript works the same way. At runtime, it analyzes the code character by character and parses out the corresponding machine code. For example: when you type
, the interpreter expects that the fifth through eighth characters will be
. This process is called tokenizing. So you can see the interpreter analyzes things line by line, character by character.
Once tokenizing is done, it moves into the AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) stage, where the whole program gets defined, and finally the code is turned into machine code.
The course also introduces a little tool called
to help you understand what tokenizing and the AST actually are.
First, let's paste the following code into Esprima:
In the Token tab on the side, you can see the code has been broken down into a bunch of object-like structures. That's what tokenizing is.
You'll probably see these options under
But one thing to note here: at the tokenizing stage, it doesn't yet know that
declares a function. It's only parsing out the words at this point.
Also, if you check "Index-based range" and "Line and column-based" at the bottom, you can see the related indexes and line numbers.
There was an unexpected hiccup here — for some reason the Tree wouldn't load for me, so I had to grab a screenshot from the video instead.
From this process you can see that the JavaScript interpreter only really starts to understand the meaning of each word at the Tree stage — for example,

means declaring a variable, and so on. But JavaScript still hasn't actually started running anything here. It's just understanding the words. The real execution happens at the code generation stage. And because every execution environment is different, the results differ too — kind of like the difference between running in a Node.js environment versus a browser environment.
The key takeaway here is that at the AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) stage, the code hasn't run yet. The actual execution happens at code generation.

This is the first post in a series on JavaScript core concepts. We kick things off with how JavaScript actually runs under the hood — what an interpreted language is, how it differs from a compiled one, and what the interpreter does step by step: tokenizing, building the AST, and finally generating code.
